Ke8 8.Qh5+! Imprecise is 8.Qe5+?! Qe7 9.Qxh8 Qxe4++– with some slight counterplay. 8...Ke7 9.Qe5++– White picks up the rook on h8.

A surprisingly common theme in Lasker's games. 7...d5 7...fxe5 8.Qh5+ g6 (8...Ke7 9.Qxe5 mate) 9.Qxe5+ Qe7 10.Qxh8+– ✓ 8.Qh5+ Or just as good is: 8.Re1 fxe5 9.Rxe5+ Kd7 10.Bg5!+– 8...g6 One source gives this game as played in New York 1911, with 8...Ke7 9.Nf7? Qe8? 0–1 (??) as the final moves, none of which makes any sense. 9.Nxg6 hxg6 10.Qxh8 ✓ Or 10.Re1+ first. 10...dxc4 There are many ways to win and you don't have to decide in advance. If you chose a slower way to advance the pawn on the 32nd move, such as 32...Bf6 or 32...Qa7+, you also get full points. In general I consider that in chess everything rests on tactics. If one thinks of strategy as a block of marble, then tactics are the chisel with which a master operates, in creating works of chess art.

1–0 If the king moves, 25.Qe5+ picks up the rook on h8 (and mates). If the bishop is taken then 25.Qf8+ mates. And finally, anything put in the way will just be taken. Not 24.Rd6? Qb1+ 25.Bf1 Qh7 26.Bg2 Qb1+=. Never play for the win, never play for the draw, just play chess!

22...Bxe3+? 23.Bxe3 wins for White. 23.Rxe3 Other moves are no better: 23.Bxe3 Qxa1–+ or 23...Bxe3+ 24.Qxe3 Qxa1–+. White is so tied up that basically any move wins by eventually bringing one rook into the action. 23...Rfe8 0–1 Chess is like body-building. If you train every day, you stay in top shape. It is the same with your brain – chess is a matter of daily training. In any match, there are few critical moments where there's no second- best decision. The rest of the moves are intuitive.

metadata.gameSource: Magnus Carlsen - Sergey Karjakin, New York (rapid 4) 2016
metadata.comment: 1–0 What a way to finish a World Championship! It's mate in one however Black takes back. There are some aspects of work you need to keep working on and no matter what environment you are in. Continuous learning is very important. It's what I call 'competitive tension', which is about having a competition around. – Viswanathan Anand
